name: Cancho Blanco and Montánchez Climbs Loop
name_original: Subida a la Estación de Radar Cancho Blanco — circular desde Alcuéscar
description: Tackle the difficult 48.4-mile road cycling route to the Cancho Blanco Radar Station from Alcuéscar, gaining 5236 feet for panoramic views.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Alcuéscar traverse a landscape characterized by rolling hills and rural roads, offering varied terrain for cyclists. The region features a mix of open countryside and areas with historical landmarks like castles. Elevation gains are common, providing opportunities for challenging climbs. This environment supports diverse road cycling experiences, from moderate loops to more demanding routes. Best road cycling routes around Alcuéscar The most popular road cycling route is Cancho Blanco and Montánchez Climbs Loop, a difficult 48.4 miles (77.9 km) trail that takes 4 hours 54 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain and challenging climbs through the Montánchez area. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Casas de Don Antonio y Albalá desde Alcuéscar – circular por Castillo de Montánchez, a moderate 31.7 miles (51.0 km) path. This circular route passes by the historic Castillo de Montánchez, offering scenic views. Local road cyclists also love the View of Montánchez Castle – Montánchez Castle loop from Albalá, a 21.4 miles (34.5 km) trail leading through rural landscapes with views of Montánchez Castle, often completed in about 1 hour 42 minutes. Road cycling around Alcuéscar is defined by rural roads, rolling hills, and historical landmarks. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to challenging road bike climbs Alcuéscar. The routes in Alcuéscar are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 5 reviews. More than 137 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Alcuéscar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Alcuéscar?
A: There are over 10 road cycling routes available around Alcuéscar, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. The region is known for its rolling hills and rural roads.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Alcuéscar?
A: Road cycling routes around Alcuéscar primarily feature &lt;strong&gt;rolling hills and rural roads&lt;/strong&gt;. You'll encounter varied terrain, from open countryside to areas with historical landmarks. Elevation gains are common, providing opportunities for challenging climbs.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes with significant climbs in Alcuéscar?
A: Yes, Alcuéscar offers routes with significant climbs. For a demanding ride, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1190850462/cancho-blanco-and-montanchez-climbs-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cancho Blanco and Montánchez Climbs Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is a difficult 48.4-mile (77.9 km) route featuring over 1,500 meters of elevation gain through the Montánchez area.

Q: Are there any moderate road cycling routes suitable for those looking for a less strenuous ride?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in the region are moderate. A popular choice is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1058788262/casas-de-don-antonio-y-albala-desde-alcuescar-circular-por-castillo-de-montanchez" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casas de Don Antonio y Albalá desde Alcuéscar – circular por Castillo de Montánchez&lt;/a&gt;, a 31.7-mile (51.0 km) circular path that passes by the historic Castillo de Montánchez with manageable elevation.

Q: What are the best times of year to go road cycling in Alcuéscar?
A: The best times for road cycling in Alcuéscar are typically spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) when temperatures are milder. Summers can be very hot, making early morning or late afternoon rides preferable, while winters are generally mild but can be damp.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes around Alcuéscar?
A: Yes, many routes in the Alcuéscar region are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39556929" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Montánchez Castle – Montánchez Castle loop from Albalá&lt;/a&gt; is a 21.4-mile (34.5 km) circular route offering scenic views of the castle and rural landscapes.

Q: What can I see along the road cycling routes in Alcuéscar?
A: The routes often pass by historical landmarks and natural features. You can expect views of the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/745456" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montánchez Castle&lt;/a&gt;, and some routes might take you near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2990429" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cornalvo Dam&lt;/a&gt; or other scenic lakes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3071953" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cornalvo Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Alcuéscar?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Reviewers often praise the &lt;strong&gt;varied terrain&lt;/strong&gt;, the opportunity to explore &lt;strong&gt;rural roads and historical landmarks&lt;/strong&gt;, and the diverse options for different ability levels.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of Montánchez Castle?
A: Yes, several routes provide excellent views of Montánchez Castle. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39556929" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Montánchez Castle – Montánchez Castle loop from Albalá&lt;/a&gt; is specifically designed to showcase this landmark, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1058788262/casas-de-don-antonio-y-albala-desde-alcuescar-circular-por-castillo-de-montanchez" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casas de Don Antonio y Albalá desde Alcuéscar – circular por Castillo de Montánchez&lt;/a&gt; also passes by it.

Q: Are there any longer road cycling routes for endurance training around Alcuéscar?
A: For longer rides, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1190850462/cancho-blanco-and-montanchez-climbs-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cancho Blanco and Montánchez Climbs Loop&lt;/a&gt; covers nearly 48.4 miles (77.9 km) with significant elevation, making it an excellent choice for endurance training. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31430790" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Drinking Water Fountain loop from Alcuéscar&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 43.2 miles (69.6 km).

Q: What are some less-trafficked or 'hidden gem' road cycling routes in the Alcuéscar region?
A: While all routes offer a sense of rural tranquility, for a potentially quieter experience, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31430519" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Aldea del Cano to Albalá loop from Alcuéscar&lt;/a&gt;. This moderate 33.3-mile (53.6 km) route takes you through charming villages and open landscapes, often with fewer cyclists.
tips: Quite a tough climb towards the Cancho Blanco Radar Station from Zarza de Montánchez. Almost 7 km of ascent, the first 2 kilometers are quite bearable, then it becomes more difficult.
If you go with gravel or mountain bike you can go up to the antennas, the highest point.

Alcuéscar is a small town in the Sierra de Montánchez region on the border with the province of Badajoz. The city was established in 830 under the Muslim rule of the Iberian Peninsula. The Vía de la Plata, which connects Seville with Astorga, crosses the town.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This challenging road cycling route, covering nearly 78 kilometers with significant elevation gain, typically takes around 4 hours and 54 minutes to complete for experienced cyclists.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this route, and is it suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a difficult road ride and demands very good fitness. It is not suitable for beginners due to its length, considerable elevation gain, and tough climb to the radar station.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Ascent to the Cancho Blanco Radar Station route?
faqs.answer: The majority of the route is well-paved and generally easy to ride. However, be prepared for some segments that might be unpaved and difficult, especially closer to the radar station. For reaching the absolute highest point where the antennas are located, a gravel or mountain bike is recommended over a road bike.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The primary highlight is the demanding Climb to the Radar itself, offering panoramic views. You'll also pass by the View of Montánchez Castle and the Sanctuary of the Virgin of the Mountain of Alcuéscar.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail in Alcuéscar?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Alcuéscar. You should be able to find suitable parking within the town to begin your circular journey. Specific parking areas are not detailed, but Alcuéscar serves as the primary access point.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Cáceres region experiences varied seasons. For challenging rides like this, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures, avoiding the extreme heat of summer or potential chill of winter. Always check the weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route or the Sierra de Montánchez area?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are mentioned for cycling this route or accessing the general Sierra de Montánchez area. However, always respect local regulations and private property.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly or suitable for families?
faqs.answer: This route is a difficult road cycling challenge, not typically suited for dogs or families with young children. The significant distance, elevation, and potential for unpaved sections make it more appropriate for experienced adult cyclists.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers sections of the [CIMA CC06] Radar de Cancho Blanco, [CIMA CC04] Montánchez (antenas), Ruta del Robledo, and the Cordel de Merida a Trujillo, among others.

faqs.question: What essential gear should I bring for this challenging road ride?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, essential gear includes a well-maintained road bike (or gravel/mountain bike if you plan to reach the very top of the radar station), appropriate cycling apparel, helmet, plenty of water and nutrition, a repair kit, and a GPS device with the route loaded. Consider extra layers depending on the weather.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or pubs available along the route or in Alcuéscar?
faqs.answer: While specific establishments along the route are not detailed, Alcuéscar, as the starting and ending point, is a town where you can expect to find cafes and pubs for refreshments before or after your ride. It's advisable to carry sufficient supplies for the ride itself.

waypoints.name: Sanctuary of the Virgin of the Mountain of Alcuéscar
